pattern: Every element only ever has three fates -- unused, joining the first subsequence, or joining the second -- so tracking counts keyed by the pair of running GCDs those two subsequences currently have turns an exponential search into a manageable dynamic program.
hints: Think of scanning the array once and deciding, for each element, one of three things: leave it out, add it to seq1, or add it to seq2 -- the whole answer is a count over every way of making those decisions.

The only information that matters about seq1 and seq2 so far is their current running GCD, so keep a table of (gcd of seq1 so far, gcd of seq2 so far) -> number of ways to reach that state, updating it as each element is considered for all three fates.
At the end, only states where both running GCDs are equal AND non-zero (meaning both subsequences ended up non-empty) count toward the answer -- sum those counts, modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, many pairs share GCD 1
statement example, every element is identical
smallest possible input, no valid disjoint pair exists
two equal elements at the maximum value
pairwise coprime values force every pair's GCD down to 1
a mix of a common factor and a coprime outlier

stress.description: moderate-size arrays drawn from a small value range so many GCD collisions occur
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 200))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 200) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
